turns.content: I have a feature flag system using nested if statements with multiple conditional attributes, but it's getting messy. I tried using the standard cfg attribute logic directly in my code and ended up with deeply nested logic that is hard to read.

That is indeed problematic for maintainability. The cfg_if crate provides a macro called cfg_if::cfg which allows you to chain multiple conditional checks cleanly instead of nesting if statements. You can define your conditions as separate items like feature_a and feature_b, then use the macro to generate the appropriate code based on whether those features are enabled.

Actually, I think I misunderstood how it works. I thought I could just write a simple cfg_if block in my code that checks multiple flags at once without needing to define separate items first. It seems like I can just plug this into any existing function and have the macro handle everything automatically.

You cannot do that directly because the macro requires pre-defined condition names. You must declare each conditional as a separate variable or item in your code before you can use them in the cfg_if chain. This is necessary so the macro knows exactly which features to check for, and it generates the correct if/else logic based on those specific identifiers.

I see now that I need to define items first, but I was hoping to just inline everything into one complex statement like a single conditional expression. It feels like there should be a way to write all these checks in a single line without the boilerplate of defining separate variables upfront.

There is no single-line syntax for this because each condition must have an explicit name that the macro references when generating code. If you want cleaner syntax, you can use cfg_if::if_with_name which allows you to check multiple conditions in a chain but still requires named definitions beforehand. The crate avoids nested attribute logic by delegating the branching to generated code rather than relying on repeated if statements.

That clarifies it significantly. So I cannot avoid defining separate items even though I wanted everything in one place, and that's why my initial approach failed. Does this mean there is no way to write complex multi-feature checks without breaking apart the logic into multiple named components?

Yes, you must break down your conditions into named components because the macro operates on those identifiers rather than evaluating arbitrary expressions inline. This design ensures that every conditional check has a clear name and can be modified independently later without touching the entire code block. The cfg_if crate prioritizes explicit names over implicit evaluation to keep the generated logic predictable and maintainable.

Okay, I understand now. So the trade-off is between having cleaner macro-generated logic versus needing upfront definitions for each feature flag. Is there a way to automate this process so I don't have to manually name every single condition?

Not directly within the crate itself since it relies on explicit names, but you can use build scripts or macros to generate those definitions automatically based on your configuration files. Alternatively, you could consider using the cfg_aliases crate to manage feature flags more cleanly before passing them into your conditional logic.
